network.overall_strategy: Arginine is one of the most challenging prebiotic synthesis targets — no standalone experiment has produced it under plausible prebiotic conditions (Miller 1986; Pol et al. 2022). The network centers on the Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic homologation pathway, the only detailed mechanistic proposal, while incorporating multiple convergent routes to key hub molecules (HCN, cyanamide, β-aminopropionitrile, NH₃). Hydrothermal environments generate foundational C1 species (formate, formamide → HCN) and urea; surface UV-driven cyanosulfidic chemistry performs the critical homologation and guanidinylation steps; and biochemical conditions enable the final hydrolysis to arginine. A speculative but literature-cited alternative via ornithine guanidinylation is also included. The network acknowledges that arginine's prebiotic origin remains an open problem (Pol et al. 2022).

reactions.name: CO₂ hydrogenation to formate on greigite

conditions.other: Alkaline hydrothermal vent, pH 9-11, H₂-rich serpentinization fluid

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ adsorbs on greigite [Fe₄S₄]-cluster analog surface, undergoes two-electron reduction by H₂ to yield formate (HCOO⁻). Greigite's structural similarity to biological [Fe₄S₄] clusters enables this CO₂ fixation.
reactions.reasoning: Greigite-catalyzed CO₂ reduction to formate is well-established in hydrothermal prebiotic chemistry. This is the first step of carbon fixation analogous to the acetyl-CoA pathway (Preiner et al., 2020; Roldan et al., 2015). Provides the C1 building block for subsequent nitrile synthesis.

reactions.name: Formamide synthesis from formate and ammonia

conditions.other: Slightly alkaline pH, mineral surface condensation

reactions.mechanism: Ammonium formate (NH₄⁺ + HCOO⁻) undergoes thermal condensation on FeS mineral surfaces to yield formamide (HCONH₂) with loss of H₂O. The mineral surface lowers the activation barrier for the dehydration step.
reactions.reasoning: Ammonium formate → formamide is a well-known thermal condensation, demonstrated at elevated temperatures relevant to hydrothermal systems. Saladino et al. (2012) have extensively studied formamide as a key prebiotic intermediate. FeS minerals can catalyze this condensation at hydrothermal temperatures.

reactions.name: Thermal dehydration of formamide to HCN

conditions.other: Subaerial volcanic/hydrothermal boundary zone, mineral-mediated dehydration

reactions.mechanism: Formamide undergoes thermally-driven dehydration (HCONH₂ → HCN + H₂O) on mineral surfaces at elevated temperatures. This is a well-characterized pyrolysis reaction accelerated by oxide and sulfide mineral catalysts.
reactions.reasoning: Formamide thermal decomposition to HCN is a well-established reaction studied extensively by Saladino et al. (2001, 2012). Metal oxide and sulfide minerals (including TiO₂, pyrite, magnetite) catalyze this conversion at 400-500K. This provides a robust hydrothermal source of HCN, which is the central precursor for the cyanosulfidic pathway to arginine (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Atmospheric electric discharge synthesis of HCN

conditions.catalyst: atmospheric lightning / UV + TiO₂ photocatalysis at surface
conditions.other: Early Earth reducing atmosphere (N₂/CH₄/H₂), electrical discharge or intense UV

reactions.mechanism: Nitrogen and methane react under electric discharge (lightning) or extreme UV to form HCN and H₂. HCN dissolves in rainwater and accumulates in surface pools. TiO₂ mineral surfaces can also photocatalytically promote N₂/CH₄ → HCN conversion.
reactions.reasoning: HCN production from N₂/CH₄ mixtures under electrical discharge is one of the most firmly established prebiotic reactions, demonstrated by Miller (1953) and Sanchez et al. (1966). Ferris & Hagan (1984) showed that HCN accumulates in surface water bodies at prebiotically relevant concentrations. This provides an independent, atmospheric route to HCN complementing the hydrothermal formamide pathway (Miller, 1986).

reactions.name: Urea synthesis from CO₂ and ammonia

conditions.other: Excess NH₃, high pressure conditions favoring ammonium carbamate → urea dehydration

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ reacts with 2 equivalents of NH₃ to form ammonium carbamate (NH₂COONH₄), which undergoes thermal dehydration to urea (NH₂CONH₂) + H₂O. High pressure and mineral surfaces favor the equilibrium toward urea.
reactions.reasoning: The industrial Wöhler-Basarov synthesis of urea from ammonium carbamate operates at high temperature and pressure, conditions plausibly achieved in hydrothermal systems. Prebiotic urea synthesis has been discussed by Menor-Salván & Marín-Yaseli (2012). Urea serves as precursor to cyanamide, the critical guanidinylating agent in the arginine pathway.

reactions.name: Cu(I)-photocatalyzed synthesis of acrylonitrile

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox cycle with H₂S as terminal reductant
conditions.other: UV irradiation (~254 nm), shallow evaporitic pool, H₂S present

reactions.mechanism: HCN and acetylene undergo Cu(I)-mediated reductive cross-coupling under UV irradiation. Cu(I) is photo-oxidized to Cu(II), which is reduced back by H₂S, completing the catalytic cycle. The net reaction is HCN + C₂H₂ → CH₂=CHCN (acrylonitrile). Yields reported >25%.
reactions.reasoning: This is a key reaction in the Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic protometabolism (Nature Chemistry 7, 301–307). They demonstrated Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox-mediated coupling of HCN and acetylene to produce acrylonitrile at >25% yield under UV irradiation with H₂S as reductant. This is the entry point for the arginine carbon skeleton construction.

reactions.name: Conjugate addition of ammonia to acrylonitrile

conditions.other: Neutral to slightly basic pH, aqueous solution

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia performs a Michael-type conjugate addition across the C=C double bond of acrylonitrile (CH₂=CHCN), yielding β-aminopropionitrile (NH₂CH₂CH₂CN). This is a facile reaction in aqueous ammonia solution, enhanced by clay-surface concentration of reactants.
reactions.reasoning: The Michael addition of NH₃ to acrylonitrile is a well-known, facile reaction that proceeds readily at room temperature in aqueous ammonia solution. Patel et al. (2015) incorporate this step in their pathway. Montmorillonite clay enhances the reaction by concentrating both NH₃ and acrylonitrile on its surface, as demonstrated for related amine-nitrile chemistry.

reactions.name: Dehydration of urea to cyanamide on clay

conditions.other: Evaporitic drying phase, subaerial geothermal field

reactions.mechanism: Urea undergoes thermally-driven dehydration (NH₂CONH₂ → NH₂CN + H₂O) on clay mineral surfaces during the dry phase of wet-dry cycles. Montmorillonite's interlayer adsorption concentrates urea and catalyzes water elimination.
reactions.reasoning: Urea dehydration to cyanamide is a documented thermal decomposition pathway. Clay minerals catalyze this transformation under drying conditions relevant to evaporitic surface environments. Cyanamide is the essential guanidinylating reagent in the Patel et al. (2015) arginine pathway, where it installs the =NH group onto amine intermediates.

reactions.name: Photochemical HCN dimerization to cyanamide

conditions.other: UV irradiation (~254 nm), dilute HCN solution in shallow photic pool

reactions.mechanism: Under UV irradiation, HCN undergoes photochemical isomerization/dimerization. Two molecules of HCN rearrange to produce cyanamide (NH₂CN) via H-atom transfer and N–C bond isomerization. TiO₂ mineral surfaces enhance this photochemistry. This is a minor product of HCN photolysis but provides an alternative cyanamide source.
reactions.reasoning: HCN photochemistry produces a range of products including cyanamide and diaminomaleonitrile (DAMN). While oligomerization to DAMN is the dominant pathway, cyanamide formation from HCN under UV has been observed (Ferris et al., 1974; Sanchez et al., 1967). Patel et al. (2015) identify cyanamide as a key reagent that may derive from the same HCN feedstock as other intermediates in the cyanosulfidic network.

reactions.name: UV-photoreduction of β-aminopropionitrile to β-aminopropionaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox, H₂S as terminal reductant
conditions.other: UV irradiation (~300 nm), slightly acidic pH, surface evaporitic pool

reactions.mechanism: The nitrile group (–C≡N) of β-aminopropionitrile is reduced to an aldehyde (–CHO) via a two-electron, two-proton photoreduction. H₂S serves as the stoichiometric electron and hydrogen donor, oxidized to elemental sulfur. Cu(I)/Cu(II) mediates the electron transfer under UV. This yields β-aminopropionaldehyde (NH₂CH₂CH₂CHO).
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) demonstrated UV-driven photoreduction of nitriles to aldehydes using H₂S as the reductant in their cyanosulfidic network. This step converts the nitrile terminus to an aldehyde, which is essential for the subsequent cyanamide cyclization step. The reaction is part of the core Patel et al. arginine synthesis scheme (compound series leading to compound 46).

reactions.name: Cyanamide cyclization with β-aminopropionaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: β-aminopropionaldehyde reacts with cyanamide (NH₂CN) via nucleophilic addition of the terminal amine to the electrophilic carbon of cyanamide, forming a guanidinium linkage. The aldehyde then cyclizes with a nitrogen atom to form a cyclic guanidinium hemiaminal intermediate. This installs the critical guanidino moiety of arginine.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) describe this step as the key guanidinylation reaction in the arginine pathway. The β-aminopropionaldehyde rapidly adds cyanamide to form a cyclic guanidinium hemiaminal, which is the framework that ultimately becomes the guanidino group of arginine. Cyanamide's role as a prebiotic guanidinylating agent is central to this route.

reactions.name: Kiliani-Fischer homologation with HCN
reactions.mechanism: HCN adds to the aldehyde/hemiaminal carbonyl of the guanidinium intermediate via Kiliani-type cyanohydrin formation, extending the carbon chain by one unit. This adds C-4 and introduces an additional nitrile group, producing an extended guanidinium aminonitrile. The reaction proceeds in aqueous solution at mild conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) employ Kiliani–Fischer homologation (HCN addition to aldehydes) as a key chain-extension strategy throughout their cyanosulfidic network. This step extends the guanidinium intermediate toward the 5-carbon skeleton needed for arginine. Kiliani cyanohydrin formation is a classical prebiotic reaction (Oro & Kamat, 1961).

reactions.name: Final photoreduction and homologation to compound 46

conditions.other: UV irradiation (~300 nm), sequential photoreduction and HCN addition steps

reactions.mechanism: The extended guanidinium nitrile undergoes a further cycle of UV-driven photoreduction (nitrile → aldehyde using H₂S as reductant) followed by Strecker-type addition of HCN and NH₃ at the α-position. This generates the α-aminonitrile designated compound 46 by Patel et al. — the direct precursor to arginine. The compound bears a complete 5-carbon backbone with the guanidino group at the δ-position and an α-aminonitrile at C-2.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) report that iterative photoreduction/HCN addition cycles produce compound 46 (arginine α-aminonitrile) with 'uniformly good to excellent' yields at individual steps. This compound is the penultimate intermediate in the only detailed mechanistic proposal for prebiotic arginine synthesis. The UV/H₂S-driven reductions are central to the cyanosulfidic paradigm.

reactions.name: Alkaline hydrolysis of arginine α-aminonitrile to arginine

conditions.other: Alkaline pH 9-11, prolonged aqueous conditions, possible geothermal warming

reactions.mechanism: The α-aminonitrile (compound 46) undergoes stepwise hydrolysis: first, the nitrile (–C≡N) is hydrated to an amide (–CONH₂), then the amide is hydrolyzed to the carboxylic acid (–COOH). Under alkaline conditions, this proceeds to yield free arginine (2-amino-5-guanidinopentanoic acid). The hydrolysis is slow but thermodynamically favorable.
reactions.reasoning: Nitrile hydrolysis to amino acids is the final step of the classical Strecker synthesis and is well-established under mild alkaline conditions (Miller, 1957; Patel et al., 2015). The hydrolysis of compound 46 would yield arginine as described in the Patel et al. cyanosulfidic scheme. Alkaline conditions can be provided by mixing with alkaline hydrothermal fluid or by evaporitic pond chemistry. This is the kinetic bottleneck of the pathway, as nitrile hydrolysis can be slow (Miller, 1986).

reactions.name: Atmospheric synthesis of cyanoacetylene

conditions.other: Reducing early Earth atmosphere (N₂/CH₄/H₂), lightning or UV

reactions.mechanism: Cyanoacetylene (HC≡C–C≡N) forms from N₂ and CH₄ under electric discharge or intense UV radiation in the early atmosphere. It dissolves in rainwater and accumulates in surface pools alongside HCN.
reactions.reasoning: Cyanoacetylene is a well-characterized product of spark discharge experiments in N₂/CH₄ mixtures (Sanchez et al., 1966; Ferris et al., 1968). It is also detected in Titan's atmosphere and interstellar medium. In the Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic network, cyanoacetylene feeds into nucleotide precursor synthesis; here it provides an alternative entry into the acrylonitrile/β-aminopropionitrile pathway.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of cyanoacetylene to β-aminopropionitrile

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I) photoredox, H₂S as reductant, UV

reactions.mechanism: Cyanoacetylene (HC≡C–CN) undergoes conjugate addition of NH₃ to yield β-aminoacrylonitrile (H₂N–CH=CH–CN), which is then photoreduced by H₂S under UV to give β-aminopropionitrile (H₂N–CH₂–CH₂–CN). The Cu(I)/Cu(II) cycle mediates the electron transfer for the reduction of the C=C double bond.
reactions.reasoning: Michael addition of NH₃ to cyanoacetylene is analogous to its addition to acrylonitrile. The subsequent photoreduction is in the spirit of the Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic network, which uses UV/H₂S to reduce unsaturated nitriles. This provides an alternative route to β-aminopropionitrile that bypasses the acetylene → acrylonitrile step, feeding from cyanoacetylene instead.

reactions.name: Guanidinylation of ornithine by cyanamide

conditions.other: Mildly alkaline pH, concentrated pool, wet-dry cycling

reactions.mechanism: The δ-amino group of ornithine (H₂N–(CH₂)₃–CH(NH₂)–COOH) attacks the electrophilic carbon of cyanamide (NH₂CN), forming a guanidino linkage (–NH–C(=NH)–NH₂) at the δ-position. This produces arginine directly. Cyanamide is a known guanidinylating agent: it converts primary amines to guanidines via nucleophilic addition.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction represents the metabolic-analog pathway suggested by Glavin et al. (2025), where ornithine (detected in spark discharge experiments and some meteorites) reacts with cyanamide to install the guanidino group. Cyanamide is established as a prebiotic guanidinylating agent (Patel et al., 2015). While this specific reaction has not been demonstrated under prebiotic conditions, the chemistry is sound — cyanamide converts amines to guanidines readily. Pol et al. (2022) note this remains speculative but chemically plausible.

reactions.name: Cyanate oxidation from cyanamide

conditions.other: UV irradiation, shallow pool, mild oxidation

reactions.mechanism: Cyanamide (NH₂CN) undergoes hydrolysis/tautomerization to carbodiimide (HN=C=NH), which is further hydrated to cyanate (OCN⁻) under mildly oxidizing photochemical conditions. TiO₂ photocatalysis facilitates this transformation.
reactions.reasoning: Cyanamide, carbodiimide, and cyanate are interconvertible under aqueous conditions. Cyanate (OCN⁻) is a well-known prebiotic condensing agent and carbamoylating reagent (Leman et al., 2004). Its formation from cyanamide provides an alternative reactive nitrogen species for the ornithine carbamoylation route.

reactions.name: Carbamoylation of ornithine by cyanate to N-carbamoyl-ornithine

conditions.other: Concentrated pool, mild heating, excess NH₃ for guanidine conversion

reactions.mechanism: Cyanate (OCN⁻) reacts with the δ-amino group of ornithine in a carbamoylation reaction to form N⁵-carbamoyl-ornithine (citrulline analog). In the presence of excess ammonia and mild heating, the ureido group (–NH–CO–NH₂) can undergo dehydration and amination to yield the guanidino group (–NH–C(=NH)–NH₂), producing arginine. This two-step conversion (carbamoylation then guanidine formation) is thermodynamically driven by wet-dry cycling and ammonia concentration.
reactions.reasoning: Cyanate-mediated carbamoylation of amines is a well-documented reaction (Leman et al., 2004). The conversion of a ureido to guanidino group is more challenging and represents a speculative step; however, Glavin et al. (2025) discuss the ornithine → citrulline → arginine pathway as a conceivable prebiotic analog to metabolism. The use of cyanate (derived from cyanamide) rather than carbamoyl phosphate makes this more prebiotically plausible than the biological pathway. This pathway remains undemonstrated (Pol et al., 2022).

pathways.name: Full hydrothermal-to-surface cyanosulfidic pathway (urea-derived cyanamide)
pathways.description: Complete route from CO₂ through hydrothermal HCN generation, surface cyanosulfidic homologation with urea-derived cyanamide, to arginine. This is the most complete pathway tracing all carbon and nitrogen from volcanic/hydrothermal sources.

pathways.name: Atmospheric HCN with urea-derived cyanamide
pathways.description: HCN from atmospheric electric discharge feeds into the Patel cyanosulfidic pathway, with cyanamide sourced from hydrothermal urea dehydration on clay.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal HCN with HCN-derived cyanamide
pathways.description: Both HCN and cyanamide derive from the same hydrothermal HCN feedstock — HCN is produced via formamide dehydration, then used directly in homologation and also photochemically dimerized to cyanamide.

pathways.name: Atmospheric HCN with HCN-derived cyanamide (fully surface-sourced)
pathways.description: Entirely surface/atmospheric origin: N₂/CH₄ → HCN provides both the carbon backbone (via acrylonitrile) and the guanidinylating agent (via photochemical cyanamide). No hydrothermal input required.

pathways.name: Cyanoacetylene route with urea-derived cyanamide
pathways.description: Alternative carbon backbone construction via cyanoacetylene (from atmospheric chemistry) → β-aminopropionitrile, bypassing the acrylonitrile intermediate. Cyanamide from urea dehydration.

pathways.name: Cyanoacetylene route with HCN-derived cyanamide
pathways.description: Cyanoacetylene provides the backbone via β-aminopropionitrile, and HCN photolysis provides cyanamide. HCN for homologation from atmospheric or hydrothermal source.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal HCN + cyanoacetylene convergent pathway
pathways.description: HCN from hydrothermal formamide feeds into both cyanamide generation (rxn_009) and Kiliani homologation (rxn_012), while cyanoacetylene provides the C3 backbone independently. Convergent hub at β-aminopropionitrile.

pathways.name: Ornithine guanidinylation via cyanamide (metabolic-analog shortcut)
pathways.description: Speculative but literature-cited pathway: ornithine (available from spark discharge or meteoritic delivery) reacts with cyanamide to directly form arginine. Cyanamide from urea dehydration. This bypasses the complex Patel homologation entirely.

pathways.name: Ornithine carbamoylation via cyanate then guanidine conversion
pathways.description: Speculative metabolic-analog route: ornithine reacts with cyanate (derived from cyanamide hydrolysis) to form N-carbamoyl-ornithine, which converts to arginine with excess ammonia. This parallels the biological urea cycle.

pathways.name: Ornithine + HCN-derived cyanamide (fully atmospheric)
pathways.description: Atmospheric HCN photolysis provides cyanamide, which guanidinylates meteoritic/spark-discharge ornithine to form arginine. The simplest possible route with minimal steps.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The strongest pathway in the network. Faithfully recapitulates the Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic homologation route, which is the only detailed mechanistic proposal for prebiotic arginine. Excellent Hydrothermal→Surface→Biochemical progression. CO₂ hydrogenation on greigite is well-supported; formamide→HCN is classical; Cu(I) photocatalysis for acrylonitrile is experimentally validated (>25% yield). The urea→cyanamide dehydration on clay is reasonable. rxn_013 collapses multiple photoreduction/homologation steps into one node, which slightly obscures the complexity. The final alkaline hydrolysis (rxn_014) is chemically sound. Main limitation: the full sequence has never been demonstrated end-to-end in the lab, and rxn_012-013 involve speculative multi-step chemistry.
suggestions: Decompose rxn_013 into sub-steps (thiolactam formation, further reduction, final HCN addition) for greater mechanistic clarity
Add explicit yield estimates where Patel et al. provide them

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Uses atmospheric HCN from electric discharge (well-established Miller-type chemistry) combined with urea-derived cyanamide. Slightly weaker environmental progression since the hydrothermal component is limited to urea synthesis (rxn_005). The core homologation sequence is identical to P1. A viable alternative if atmospheric HCN is more available than hydrothermal formamide-derived HCN.
suggestions: Strengthen hydrothermal connection by specifying mineral catalysts for rxn_005 more explicitly
Consider whether atmospheric NH₃ source is needed

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good hydrothermal-to-surface flow. The HCN photochemical dimerization to cyanamide (rxn_009) is less well-established than urea dehydration, introducing a weaker link. Overall coherent and follows the Patel et al. framework. Nearly meets approval threshold.
suggestions: Cite specific evidence for photochemical HCN dimerization to cyanamide
Consider whether H₂CN₂ is the actual product or if aminoxazole is more likely

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Fully surface-sourced pathway with atmospheric electric discharge feeding directly into UV-driven cyanosulfidic chemistry. Lacks hydrothermal component entirely, weakening environmental diversity. The HCN→cyanamide photochemistry (rxn_009) is the weakest link. Shortest atmospheric route but less geochemically grounded.
suggestions: Add at least one hydrothermal precursor step to improve environmental realism
Specify NH₃ source for rxn_007

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The cyanoacetylene→β-aminopropionitrile step (rxn_016) is speculative. While cyanoacetylene is a known prebiotic molecule, its reductive amination to β-aminopropionitrile via H₂S is not well-documented. The rest of the pathway follows the established cyanosulfidic framework.
suggestions: Provide experimental evidence for cyanoacetylene reductive amination
Consider intermediate hydrogenation steps

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Combines the two weakest alternative routes (cyanoacetylene and HCN dimerization). Both rxn_016 and rxn_009 are under-supported. HCN source for rxn_009 is also unclear in this pathway. Functional but the least robust of the cyanosulfidic family.
suggestions: Specify HCN source clearly
This pathway would benefit from at least one well-established reaction for each critical intermediate

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Convergent pathway bringing together hydrothermal HCN and atmospheric cyanoacetylene. Good environmental diversity but inherits the weakness of rxn_016. The convergence at β-aminopropionitrile is a nice architectural feature showing network robustness.
suggestions: Focus on validating the cyanoacetylene amination step

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Ornithine guanidinylation by cyanamide is a plausible analogy to biochemistry but is experimentally undemonstrated under prebiotic conditions. The critical weakness is that ornithine (mol_021) is introduced as a starting material without any synthesis pathway within the network. This is acknowledged as speculative. Furthermore, the guanidinylation of an amine by cyanamide typically gives substituted guanidines but selectivity and yield under prebiotic conditions are unknown.
suggestions: Add a synthesis pathway for ornithine from glutamate or simpler precursors
Cite any evidence for cyanamide-mediated guanidinylation of amines

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Contains a significant chemical error: rxn_019 describes 'carbamoylation of ornithine by cyanate' producing 'N-carbamoyl-ornithine', which is citrulline (C₆H₁₃N₃O₃), NOT arginine (C₆H₁₄N₄O₂). Citrulline has a ureido group, while arginine has a guanidino group. An additional step converting citrulline to arginine (e.g., via amidination) would be needed. This pathway as written does not produce the target.
suggestions: Correct the product of rxn_019 to citrulline and add a citrulline→arginine conversion step
Or replace cyanate with cyanamide for direct guanidinylation
Add ornithine synthesis route

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Minimal pathway relying on atmospheric HCN dimerization to cyanamide plus ornithine guanidinylation. Same issues as P8 (ornithine not synthesized within network, guanidinylation undemonstrated) compounded with the weaker HCN→cyanamide route. Speculative but provides network redundancy.
suggestions: Add ornithine synthesis
Strengthen evidence for HCN dimerization pathway

evaluation.network_feedback: This is an exceptionally well-designed synthesis network for one of the most challenging prebiotic targets. The network faithfully and rigorously follows the Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic homologation pathway, which is the only detailed mechanistic proposal for prebiotic arginine synthesis. Key strengths: (1) Excellent topology with 22 molecules, 19 reactions, and 5 well-connected hub molecules (HCN, cyanamide, β-aminopropionitrile, NH₃, H₂S). (2) Strong convergent architecture with multiple routes to key intermediates (2 routes to HCN, 2 to cyanamide, 2 to β-aminopropionitrile). (3) Clear Hydrothermal→Surface→Biochemical environmental flow in the primary pathways. (4) Honest acknowledgment that arginine has never been produced prebiotically (Miller 1986; Pol et al. 2022). (5) Strong literature alignment with citations to Patel et al. 2015, Miller 1986, Glavin et al. 2025, and Pol et al. 2022. Weaknesses: (1) rxn_013 collapses multiple complex steps into one node. (2) The ornithine pathways (P8-P10) are weak because ornithine has no synthesis route in the network. (3) P9 contains a chemical error (carbamoylation produces citrulline, not arginine). (4) Some reaction molecular formulas need verification (mol_014-mol_016). (5) The cyanoacetylene→β-aminopropionitrile route (rxn_016) lacks experimental support. Despite these issues, the primary pathway (P1) achieves the approval threshold, and the network as a whole represents the best possible synthesis proposal for this notoriously difficult target.
